metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the spy target and assertion for `test_capability_check_cached` (e.g., 'patch `runner._run_capability_check` and assert it is called exactly once across two invocations with the same model string, with `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` inspected directly as a secondary assertion').
findings.finding: The AC for `test_capability_check_cached` says the check is 'invoked once per model string, asserted by a spy' but does not specify WHAT the spy instruments — which function or method is patched, and what call-count assertion is made. An executor must invent the spy target and the assertion form.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — `test_capability_check_cached`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Gate the usage-plausibility WARNING on `self._model_override is None` (mirroring the existing pattern on line 203) so it never fires on the offline test path — preserving the signal-to-noise ratio of the #5360 observability.
findings.finding: The usage-plausibility WARNING (input_tokens==0 AND output_tokens==0 AND non-empty result) will trigger on every offline test that uses `model_override` (FunctionModel/TestModel), because `_extract_usage` already returns all-zero dicts for test models that carry no real usage. This produces spurious WARNINGs in the normal test suite and would require caplog noise filtering in tests not related to this invariant.
findings.location: What (concrete mechanisms) — 'Usage-plausibility telemetry WARNING' section; also runner.py _extract_usage (lines 574-605)
findings.suggested_fix: In the post-run plausibility check in runner.py, wrap it: `if self._model_override is None and usage.get('input_tokens', 0) == 0 and usage.get('output_tokens', 0) == 0 and <non_empty_result>: logger.warning(...)`. This matches the guard pattern already used for `assert_gated` and the anthropic model construction.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Ground `model.profile.supports_tools` in a verifiable external source: add a link to the pydantic-ai 1.107.0 docs/source for `ModelProfile`, or commit a minimal offline script (like the existing POC) that imports `AnthropicModel`, accesses `.profile.supports_tools`, and prints the value — making the capability assertion independently reproducible (`## Verified library behavior`).
findings.finding: The plan's central construction-time capability check is built entirely on `model.profile.supports_tools` — claimed as a 'VERIFIED pydantic-ai 1.107.0 signal' — but there is zero evidence of this attribute in any repo file (codebase search returns no hits), no external documentation URL, no committed POC snippet, and no pydantic-ai source pointer. The only support is the plan's own inline experiment log, which cannot be independently verified.
findings.location: ## What (concrete mechanisms) — Construction-time provider tool-calling check; ## Verified library behavior (experiment log — pydantic-ai 1.107.0, offline)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan's Verified library behavior section: a pydantic-ai source reference (e.g. `pydantic_ai/models/__init__.py` `ModelProfile` class) and/or a two-line reproducible snippet: `from pydantic_ai.models.anthropic import AnthropicModel; m = AnthropicModel('claude-opus-4-8', ...); assert hasattr(m, 'profile') and hasattr(m.profile, 'supports_tools')`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix the guard expression specification to use `hasattr(model, 'profile') and hasattr(model.profile, 'supports_tools')` (two-step) so the check is safely skipped for any model object without a `.profile` attribute, matching the stated NO-OP contract.
findings.finding: The capability-check guard expression `hasattr(model.profile, 'supports_tools')` as specified in the plan evaluates `model.profile` as a Python sub-expression before `hasattr` can protect it. If any non-standard model adapter exposes a `.profile` property that raises on access (AttributeError or otherwise), the guard itself crashes rather than gracefully skipping the check. This is a structural gap in the specified decision rule for the guard protocol.
findings.location: What section — 'Construction-time provider tool-calling check'; guard expression `not isinstance(model, str) and hasattr(model.profile, 'supports_tools')`
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `hasattr(model.profile, 'supports_tools')` with `hasattr(model, 'profile') and hasattr(model.profile, 'supports_tools')` in the plan's guard expression.

findings.finding: `model.profile.supports_tools` is asserted as a 'VERIFIED pydantic-ai 1.107.0 signal' — the entire construction-time capability check is built on this attribute — but there are zero hits for `profile` or `supports_tools` anywhere in the rebar codebase (no import, no usage, no test). The only evidence is the plan's own inline experiment log, making this empirically unverifiable from the repo.
findings.location: What (concrete mechanisms) — 'Construction-time provider tool-calling check'; AC: `test_capability_check_fails_fast`
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line import+assertion probe in `tests/unit/test_agent_build_invariants.py` that instantiates an `AnthropicModel` stub (or reads `AnthropicModel.__init__` signature) and asserts `.profile` has `supports_tools`, so the test suite itself catches any future library rename. Also cite the specific pydantic-ai source file/line (e.g. `pydantic_ai/models/anthropic.py`) in the plan's verified-behavior log.

findings.finding: The model_override (test-double) exclusion from the construction-time capability check relies on an implicit mechanism not stated in the plan. The plan says the check is a NO-OP for `model_override` but the described guard `not isinstance(model, str)` is TRUE for any pydantic-ai TestModel (which is not a string), so the check body WOULD be entered for a test double. The only thing that would save it is TestModel lacking a `.profile` attribute (the `hasattr(model.profile, 'supports_tools')` guard), but the plan presents the NO-OP as a structural property of the check, not as an implicit hasattr fallback. The plan needs to specify an explicit structural exclusion (e.g. `self._model_override is None`) as part of the guard condition, or acknowledge that `.profile` absence on TestModel is the intended exclusion mechanism.
findings.location: What section – Construction-time provider tool-calling check; AC line `test_capability_check_noop_for_string_model`
findings.suggested_fix: Amend the guard description to: `self._model_override is None and not isinstance(model, str) and hasattr(model, 'profile') and hasattr(model.profile, 'supports_tools')` — making the model_override exclusion structural and explicit, independent of whether the test double's type ever gains a `.profile`.

findings.finding: The plan's central capability assertion — that `model.profile.supports_tools` is a reliable bool attribute on a pydantic-ai `AnthropicModel` object in version 1.107.0 — is cited only from a self-contained inline experiment log with no external anchor (no GitHub source URL, no API docs link, no changelog reference). There are zero hits for `supports_tools` or `model.profile` anywhere in the codebase. This is the sole mechanism behind the construction-time guard; a wrong attribute name silently makes the check a permanent NO-OP (the plan's own `hasattr` guard suppresses AttributeError).
findings.location: What (concrete mechanisms) — 'Construction-time provider tool-calling check'; Verified library behavior section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the test file: `from pydantic_ai.models.anthropic import AnthropicModel; m = AnthropicModel('claude-3-haiku-20240307', provider=...); assert hasattr(m, 'profile') and isinstance(m.profile.supports_tools, bool)` — this pins the attribute contract and fails loudly if pydantic-ai renames it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the single `hasattr(model.profile, 'supports_tools')` expression with a two-step guard `hasattr(model, 'profile') and hasattr(model.profile, 'supports_tools')` (or wrap in a try/except AttributeError returning False) so the check is safely a no-op for any model object that does not expose the expected profile shape.
findings.finding: The plan's construction-time guard expression `hasattr(model.profile, 'supports_tools')` accesses `model.profile` as a Python sub-expression before `hasattr` can protect it. If any non-string model object lacks a `.profile` attribute (or exposes it as a property that raises a non-AttributeError exception), the guard itself throws unexpectedly rather than silently no-oping, potentially crashing agent construction for any future or third-party model type not validated against pydantic-ai 1.107.0.
findings.location: What (concrete mechanisms) — Construction-time provider tool-calling check; `src/rebar/llm/runner.py`
findings.suggested_fix:
Change the guard from:
```python
not isinstance(model, str) and hasattr(model.profile, 'supports_tools')
```
to:
```python
not isinstance(model, str) and hasattr(model, 'profile') and hasattr(model.profile, 'supports_tools')
```
This makes the entire check a guaranteed no-op when the profile shape is absent, regardless of future library changes.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before implementing the `model.profile.supports_tools` capability check, add a verifiable external anchor for this attribute (e.g. pydantic-ai 1.107.0 GitHub source link or PyPI changelog entry), or add a spike test that `assert hasattr(AnthropicModel(...).profile, 'supports_tools')` passes with the pinned version, and cite it in the plan.
findings.finding: `model.profile.supports_tools` is asserted as a 'VERIFIED pydantic-ai 1.107.0 signal' — the entire construction-time capability check is built on this attribute — yet there is zero codebase usage of `.profile` on a model object, and the codebase's own structured.py (lines 40–46) explicitly defers depending on the pydantic-ai model profile, commenting it is 'until the profile is confirmed.' The evidence is the plan's own inline experiment log with no external verifiable anchor (no PyPI docs URL, no GitHub source citation, no changelog reference).
findings.location: What (concrete mechanisms) — 'Construction-time provider tool-calling check'; Verified library behavior section; AC: `test_capability_check_fails_fast`
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line spike test to the plan's verification log section: `from pydantic_ai.models.anthropic import AnthropicModel; m = AnthropicModel('claude-3-5-haiku-20241022'); assert hasattr(m, 'profile') and hasattr(m.profile, 'supports_tools')` — run this against the pinned `pydantic-ai-slim>=1,<2` in CI and record the output as the verifiable anchor. If the attribute is absent, name the actual bool signal that pydantic-ai does expose for tool-calling capability (e.g. check the `ModelProfile` dataclass source in pydantic-ai for the correct field name).

findings.finding: `model.profile.supports_tools` is asserted as a 'VERIFIED pydantic-ai 1.107.0 signal' but has zero codebase usage and the only evidence is the plan's own inline experiment log — no external anchor (source-code path, GitHub link, or importable symbol) is cited. The pyproject.toml pins `pydantic-ai-slim>=1,<2` (not `==1.107.0`), so the attribute's existence must hold across the entire `>=1,<2` range for the construction-time check to be safe; a missing or renamed attribute on any in-range version would cause an `AttributeError` crash on the Anthropic path (not a graceful `LLMConfigError`).
findings.location: What section — 'Construction-time provider tool-calling check'; AC: `test_capability_check_fails_fast` / `test_capability_check_noop_for_string_model` / `test_capability_check_cached`
findings.suggested_fix: In the implementation, wrap the attribute access defensively: `if hasattr(model, 'profile') and hasattr(model.profile, 'supports_tools') and model.profile.supports_tools is False: raise LLMConfigError(...)` — this already matches the plan's stated guard condition (`hasattr(model.profile, 'supports_tools')`), but make sure the plan's AC tests also verify the `AttributeError`-safe path (i.e. a model object with no `.profile` attribute is a no-op, not a crash). Additionally, cite a concrete import path for `model.profile` from the installed pydantic-ai package to nail the evidence class to Verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the 'non-empty result' predicate for the usage-plausibility warning per output mode (e.g., for `text`: non-empty string; for `structured`/`findings`: result dict has at least one substantive key beyond runner/model/trace_id), and add this rule to the AC for `test_usage_plausibility_warns_never_blocks`.
findings.finding: The 'non-empty result' predicate that gates the usage-plausibility WARNING is a referenced decision rule that is never defined. The plan body says the warning fires 'while the run produced a non-empty result', but this condition is absent from the AC for test_usage_plausibility_warns_never_blocks, and runner.py has three structurally distinct output branches (text → {text,...}, structured → validated payload dict, findings → review_result pipeline) with no shared 'non-empty' concept. An implementer must guess what 'non-empty' means for each mode.
findings.location: What section — 'Usage-plausibility telemetry WARNING'; AC line 'test_usage_plausibility_warns_never_blocks'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'What' section: 'Non-empty is defined as: for mode=text, the result text is a non-empty string; for mode=structured or findings, the result dict contains at least one key beyond {runner, model, trace_id}.' Mirror this definition in the AC.

findings.finding: The 'non-empty result' predicate that gates the usage-plausibility WARNING is referenced as a decision rule but never defined. runner.py has three distinct output-mode branches (text → SimpleNamespace message list, structured → a validated model instance, findings → finalize_outcome dict), and 'non-empty' means something different for each. The plan provides no concrete predicate (e.g., 'output string is truthy', 'structured payload is not None', 'findings list len > 0') co-located with the warning logic, leaving the implementer to guess.
findings.location: What section — 'Usage-plausibility telemetry WARNING'; AC line 'test_usage_plausibility_warns_never_blocks'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: 'non-empty result means: for mode=text, the output string is non-empty; for mode=structured, the returned object is not None; for mode=findings, the finalize_outcome result contains at least one key beyond _usage. The check fires AFTER finalize_outcome / _extract_usage returns.'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a fixture (e.g. `autouse` or explicit) that clears `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` before each test in `test_agent_build_invariants.py`, and document this in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan names `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` as a new module-level set to add to `runner.py`, but this symbol does not exist anywhere in the codebase. The plan also does not specify a mechanism for test isolation (resetting the set between test runs), even though `test_capability_check_cached` explicitly relies on a spy counting invocations — a module-level singleton will retain state across tests in the same process.
findings.location: What — Construction-time provider tool-calling check; Acceptance Criteria — `test_capability_check_cached`
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `@pytest.fixture(autouse=True)` in the new test module that calls `runner._TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED.clear()` before each test.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the exact cache key expression in the plan (e.g., `model.model_name` or `type(model).__name__ + ':' + model.model_name`) so the executor has no ambiguity about the 'resolved model string'.
findings.finding: The cache key for `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` is described as 'keyed by the resolved model string' but the plan never specifies what string is actually used (model class name, `.model_name` attribute, `repr(model)`, etc.). An executor must invent this detail, risking a key collision or over-invalidation that breaks the 'cached once per unique model' guarantee.
findings.location: What (concrete mechanisms) — Construction-time provider tool-calling check, CACHE paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'CACHE KEY: the string `model.model_name` (the pydantic-ai standard attribute exposed by all concrete model objects), so two `AnthropicModel("claude-3-5-sonnet")` instances share one cache entry.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact cache key for `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` — e.g., explicitly state 'use the `resolved` string returned by `_pai_model()`, captured before the model object is built, as the set key' — so the implementer and the test (test_capability_check_cached) have an unambiguous key.
findings.finding: The cache key for _TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED is described as 'keyed by the resolved model string', but the plan never specifies which string is used as the key in the anthropic path, where the resolved string (e.g. 'anthropic:claude-opus-4-8') is transformed into an AnthropicModel object before the check. The plan states the check runs on a model OBJECT (inspecting model.profile.supports_tools), so the cache must be keyed on something derivable at check-time — but the plan never says whether the key is the resolved string from _pai_model(), the model object's class name, or a model.model_name attribute.
findings.location: What section — 'Construction-time provider tool-calling check'; CACHE paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the CACHE paragraph: 'The key is the `resolved` string from `_pai_model(cfg)` (e.g. "anthropic:claude-opus-4-8"), captured before the AnthropicModel object is constructed, so the set contains provider-qualified model name strings.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align the `test_usage_plausibility_warns_never_blocks` AC with the body's full triggering condition — add 'when the run produced a non-empty result' to the AC so the guard is explicitly verified.
findings.finding: The AC for `test_usage_plausibility_warns_never_blocks` omits the 'non-empty result' condition that guards the warning in the plan body, creating a specification gap an implementer could resolve inconsistently.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — `test_usage_plausibility_warns_never_blocks` AC line vs. 'Usage-plausibility telemetry WARNING' body section
findings.suggested_fix: Change the AC to: 'Usage-plausibility emits a WARNING (asserted via caplog) when both `input_tokens == 0` and `output_tokens == 0` on a successful run that produced a non-empty result; NEVER blocks; and does NOT warn when the result is empty (`test_usage_plausibility_warns_never_blocks`).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge or enforce concurrency safety of `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED`: either add a `threading.Lock` guarding the check-then-add sequence (making 'once per process' a hard guarantee), or explicitly document that duplicate checks are benign (idempotent, read-only) and the CPython GIL makes `set.add` atomic, so the soft 'once' guarantee is acceptable.
findings.finding: The `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` module-level set is updated via a check-then-act pattern (check if model string in set → run capability check → add to set) without any concurrency guard. In a multi-threaded process, two threads constructing agents with the same model simultaneously will both find the key absent, both run the check, and both add to the set — a benign TOCTOU race in CPython (GIL protects `set.add`), but the plan does not acknowledge the threading model or specify that the operation is safe under concurrent access.
findings.location: What (concrete mechanisms) — Construction-time provider tool-calling check; `src/rebar/llm/runner.py`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC test (`test_capability_check_passes_for_capable_model_object`) asserting that a model object with `profile.supports_tools == True` used in a tool-using op does NOT raise and proceeds normally.
findings.finding: The construction-time capability check introduces a new code path that runs for every tool-using agent construction with a model object, but the acceptance criteria only specify a test for the FAILURE path (supports_tools==False → LLMConfigError). There is no AC for the SUCCESS path (model object with supports_tools==True → no raise, execution proceeds normally). An implementation that accidentally raises for all model objects would still pass all named ACs.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — `test_capability_check_fails_fast`, `test_capability_check_noop_for_string_model`, `test_capability_check_cached`
findings.suggested_fix: Add to ACs: `test_capability_check_passes_for_capable_model_object` — construct a mock model object with `profile.supports_tools = True`, call the construction path with tools non-empty, and assert no `LLMConfigError` is raised and the cached set is populated.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC test asserting that a successful run with non-zero input/output tokens does NOT emit the usage-plausibility warning (guards against a broken implementation that always warns).
findings.finding: The plan introduces a per-run usage-plausibility telemetry warning (runtime behavior on every successful run), but the title promises only 'tests + one-time capability check'. This runtime warning is minor scope beyond the stated title intent, and importantly the AC for it (`test_usage_plausibility_warns_never_blocks`) tests only the positive-warning case (all-zero usage + non-empty result → warn). The inverse case (non-zero usage → no spurious warning) is unspecified, leaving a tautological risk where a broken implementation that always warns would still pass.
findings.location: What section — 'Usage-plausibility telemetry WARNING'; AC — `test_usage_plausibility_warns_never_blocks`
findings.suggested_fix: Add `test_usage_plausibility_no_warn_on_normal_usage` — mock `_extract_usage` to return `{input_tokens: 10, output_tokens: 5}` with a non-empty result, assert no WARNING matching the plausibility pattern appears in caplog.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Scope `test_expected_tools_offered` to the filesystem_tools names only (the genuinely new coverage), and explicitly reference the existing `test_rebar_tools_are_least_privilege` as the canonical rebar_tools name guard — or extend the existing test rather than duplicating it in the new file.
findings.finding: The proposed `test_expected_tools_offered` partially rebuilds coverage that already exists in `tests/unit/test_pydantic_ai_runner.py::test_rebar_tools_are_least_privilege` (lines 274-278), which already asserts the exact `__name__` set for `rebar_tools` in both `allow_comment=False` and `allow_comment=True` modes. Creating a second test in a new file that re-asserts rebar_tools names is NIH duplication rather than extension.
findings.location: AC: `test_expected_tools_offered`; Files: `tests/unit/test_agent_build_invariants.py`
findings.suggested_fix: In `tests/unit/test_agent_build_invariants.py`, assert only `{t.__name__ for t in pai_tools.filesystem_tools('.')} == {'read_file', 'list_directory', 'search_files'}` and add a comment cross-referencing `test_rebar_tools_are_least_privilege` for rebar_tools coverage.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile `test_expected_tools_offered` with the existing `test_rebar_tools_are_least_privilege` in `test_pydantic_ai_runner.py` — either extend the existing test or explicitly note the new file is additive and explain why duplication is acceptable.
findings.finding: The plan's `test_expected_tools_offered` AC proposes asserting `__name__`s on `filesystem_tools`/`rebar_tools` in the new file, but `tests/unit/test_pydantic_ai_runner.py` already contains `test_rebar_tools_are_least_privilege` (line 274) which asserts exactly `[t.__name__ for t in read_only] == ['show_ticket']` and `{t.__name__ for t in full} == {'show_ticket', 'comment_ticket'}`. The plan neither reconciles with nor acknowledges this existing test, creating a duplication risk.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — `test_expected_tools_offered`; `tests/unit/test_pydantic_ai_runner.py:274`
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan, note that `test_expected_tools_offered` extends coverage to `filesystem_tools` names (genuinely new) and acknowledges the overlap; alternatively, move the `rebar_tools` name assertions into the new file and delete/rename the existing test.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge the existing `test_rebar_tools_are_least_privilege` and `test_filesystem_tools_are_repo_confined` coverage in the AC for `test_expected_tools_offered`, and explicitly state what this new test adds that those do not (e.g. exhaustive exact-set assertion against a hardcoded expected list, or a hook-mocking path that simulates #5177) — or consolidate into the existing test file rather than a new one.
findings.finding: The plan's `test_expected_tools_offered` AC proposes a new test asserting that `pai_tools.filesystem_tools`/`rebar_tools` return callables with exactly the expected `__name__`s. This coverage already exists in `tests/unit/test_pydantic_ai_runner.py`: `test_rebar_tools_are_least_privilege` explicitly asserts `[t.__name__ for t in read_only] == ["show_ticket"]` and `{t.__name__ for t in full} == {"show_ticket", "comment_ticket"}`; `test_filesystem_tools_are_repo_confined` exercises all three FS tool callables by name. The plan neither acknowledges this prior art nor differentiates the new test's scope.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — Test: `pai_tools.filesystem_tools`/`rebar_tools` offer exactly the expected tool names (`test_expected_tools_offered`)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either specify a cache-reset mechanism (e.g. a pytest fixture that clears `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` before each capability-check test, or expose a `_reset_capability_cache()` helper for tests) in the plan, or justify why the module-level singleton pattern is preferred over a simpler stateless inline check or `functools.lru_cache` on the check function (`## What — CACHE`).
findings.finding: The plan introduces a module-level process-lifetime mutable singleton (`_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED: set`) as a novel caching pattern without considering the simpler alternative (check is inline and cheap enough to skip caching) or the test-isolation implications (a test that populates the cache leaves the model string in the set for all subsequent tests in the same process, making `test_capability_check_cached` order-dependent and `test_capability_check_fails_fast` potentially unreachable if a prior test already cached the model string).
findings.location: ## What (concrete mechanisms) — CACHE: a module-level `set()` `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED`; ## Acceptance Criteria — `test_capability_check_cached`
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan: 'Tests that exercise the cache populate/miss path use a `autouse=False` fixture that calls `runner._TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED.clear()` in teardown (or monkeypatches the set) to guarantee test isolation.' Alternatively, replace the bare `set()` with `functools.lru_cache` on the check helper, which is resettable via `.cache_clear()` in tests.

findings.finding: The plan explicitly states the capability check is a NO-OP for `model_override` (a test double), but no Acceptance Criterion or named test covers this path. If the guard condition (`not isinstance(model, str)` and `hasattr(model.profile, 'supports_tools')`) misbehaves on a test-double object that lacks `.profile` entirely (e.g., raises AttributeError rather than silently skipping), the check would crash in test runs — contradicting the stated NO-OP guarantee — without any test catching it.
findings.location: AC section — `test_capability_check_noop_for_string_model` + plan body paragraph 'Construction-time provider tool-calling check' (model_override mention)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to ACs: `test_capability_check_noop_for_model_override` — construct a simple mock object (not a string, lacking `.profile.supports_tools`) and assert the check exits silently. Also consider tightening the guard to `isinstance(model, str) or not hasattr(model, 'profile') or not hasattr(model.profile, 'supports_tools')` to make the NO-OP behavior robust to attribute-absent test doubles.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit back-out plan or opt-out mechanism for the new `LLMConfigError` raise path in `runner.py` (e.g., an env-var bypass flag, or a note that the check can be disabled by setting `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` to skip, with a rollback procedure documented in the plan).
findings.finding: The plan adds a new `LLMConfigError` raise path in `runner.py` (the construction-time capability check) that changes existing caller behavior, but provides no rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract sequencing for that new exception path.
findings.location: What section — 'Construction-time provider tool-calling check'; Files section — `src/rebar/llm/runner.py`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a persistent documentation artifact (e.g., a CLAUDE.md entry or an inline `# Design:` block in `runner.py`) explaining the rationale for `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED`, why the check is one-time-only (not per-call), and why the string-model path is a NO-OP.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural pattern — a module-level construction-time capability cache (`_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED`), a new `LLMConfigError` raise contract at agent-build time, and the explicit 'invariants as tests + one-time check, never per-call' design decision — but specifies no persistent documentation update (ADR, CLAUDE.md entry, or inline design comment) to record the rationale in the repository.
findings.location: ## What (concrete mechanisms) — Construction-time provider tool-calling check; Files: src/rebar/llm/runner.py
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `# Design note:` comment block above `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` in `runner.py` (and optionally a bullet in CLAUDE.md) capturing: (1) why per-call is rejected, (2) why string-model paths are excluded, (3) the pydantic-ai version contract being relied on, and a reference to the bug numbers (#5177, #6186, #1987, #5360).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove or defer the `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` process-lifetime cache (the 'hot loop' claim is ungrounded — cite a profiling result or call-rate measurement if the cache is truly warranted; otherwise a plain per-call `hasattr` guard is sufficient and eliminates the test-isolation problem).
findings.finding: The `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` module-level set is justified only by 'a hot loop pays nothing,' which is an unsupported performance claim, not evidence of actual overhead. The construction-time check fires at most once per `PydanticAIRunner.run()` invocation, and the runner is not called in a tight sub-loop in any observable code path. No profiling result, call-rate measurement, or scale estimate is cited. By the G-9 / DSO premature-optimization criterion, an optimization mechanism requires affirmative evidence before being added.
findings.location: What section — 'Construction-time provider tool-calling check'; CACHE bullet; AC `test_capability_check_cached`
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the module-level set with a simple inline guard: `if not isinstance(model, str) and hasattr(model, 'profile') and hasattr(model.profile, 'supports_tools') and not model.profile.supports_tools: raise LLMConfigError(...)`. If the check is truly called in a hot path later, add the cache then with a measured justification.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the callers of `PydanticAIRunner.run()` / `get_runner()` outside `src/rebar/llm/` (e.g. `operations.py`, `review_workflows.py`, `workflow/` engine) that would encounter the new `LLMConfigError` raise path, and confirm they already handle `LLMConfigError` or note any required updates.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new `LLMConfigError` raise path inside `PydanticAIRunner.run()` (the construction-time capability check), but does not enumerate callers outside `src/rebar/llm/` that invoke `PydanticAIRunner.run()` or `get_runner()` and may encounter the new exception. Files such as `src/rebar/llm/operations.py`, `src/rebar/llm/review_workflows.py`, and the `workflow/` engine are unenumerated consumers that would surface this new exception.
findings.location: Scope section; What — Construction-time provider tool-calling check

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
